course.title: sand Onboarding: User, Developer, Maintainer
course.topic: This codebase: onboarding a new developer and a new user to use the app, and helping a developer maintain and debug it
course.mission: Help a beginner use sand safely, understand the codebase as if they had built it, make small changes test-first, and debug maintenance failures with confidence.

learnerProfile.currentLevel: Beginner with sand, Swift/XCTest, and CLI developer tooling.
learnerProfile.targetLevel: Can use sand end-to-end, explain the product/domain model, navigate the repo, trace commands through the architecture, make small tested changes, and debug common failures.
preferences: Project-based walkthroughs where each lesson builds on the previous one
Conceptual diagrams and mental models before or alongside code
Small toy examples only when they clarify the real repo
constraints: Balance Swift syntax, docs process, architecture theory, and internals by introducing each at the moment it becomes useful
Preserve sand product language and v1 boundaries

subjectProfile.domain: Swift CLI for Sandbox VM developer tooling

lessonOutline.title: Run the app like a user
lessonOutline.objective: Use the main sand command loop from doctor through delete without touching internals.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner has not used sand or knows only that it is a safer place to run tools.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can describe and rehearse the end-to-end user workflow: doctor, create, inspect, add folders, run, shell, stop/start, logs, ephemeral init/run, delete.
mustCover: What sand is in one paragraph
Prerequisites from README at a high level
The quickstart command sequence
Allowed Folder examples with read-write and read-only intent
Pi as an ordinary Workload Command
What success looks like after each command
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Build a personal dry-run checklist for using sand safely on a project.

lessonOutline.title: Draw the Sandbox VM mental model
lessonOutline.objective: Explain the product model using sand's canonical language.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner has seen the user workflow but may not know why each command exists.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can draw Host Mac, Sandbox VM, Sandbox Guest, Allowed Folders, Guest Paths, Guest State, Runtime Instance, Sandbox Session, and Workload Command relationships.
mustCover: Canonical terms from issues/sand/CONTEXT.md
Host Mac vs Sandbox Guest boundary
Allowed Folder and Access Mode model
Guest State persistence vs Runtime Instance disposability
Workload Command opacity
Outbound-only networking and OAuth callback handoff at a user-model level
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Label a small architecture sketch and explain why sand protects the Host Mac.

lessonOutline.title: Tour the repo like a maintainer
lessonOutline.objective: Map the repository into product docs, source modules, tests, scripts, and completion gates.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner understands the product vocabulary but not where the implementation lives.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can find the right file or test for a user-facing behavior without browsing randomly.
mustCover: Package.swift products and targets
Sources/sand/main.swift as composition root
Sources/SandCore module folders and responsibilities
Tests/SandCoreTests mapping to behavior areas
README, onboarding, developer guide, CLI reference, CONTEXT, ADR
Makefile gates: swift test, make docs-check, make check
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Given three hypothetical tasks, choose the first source file and first test file to inspect.

lessonOutline.title: Trace one durable command
lessonOutline.objective: Follow a normal command from CLI arguments to application request to lifecycle coordination to backend boundary.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner can name repo areas but has not followed execution flow.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can trace `sand demo run pwd` or `sand create demo` through router, request structs, LifecycleCoordinator, metadata, policies, and SandboxBackend.
mustCover: main.swift construction of metadata store, backend, coordinator, router
CLICommandRouter dispatch pattern
SandboxApplication as the application boundary
LifecycleCoordinator orchestration role
SandboxBackend as narrow adapter port
Where CommandResult becomes process exit code
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Annotate the path of one command with file names and responsibilities.

lessonOutline.title: Understand specs, folders, and working directories
lessonOutline.objective: Explain how sand decides what a Sandbox VM can see and where commands start.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner knows command flow but not the key domain policies inside it.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can reason about Sandbox Specs, Allowed Folder updates, duplicate Guest Paths, overlapping host folders, Access Modes, and Working Directory Mapping.
mustCover: SandboxSpec role and YAML surface
FolderPolicy add/update/remove rules
Default Guest Path derivation
Access Mode aliases and canonical terms
Overlap rejection and duplicate Guest Path rejection
WorkingDirectoryMapper fallback behavior
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Predict accepted/rejected folder mappings and command working directories for realistic examples.

lessonOutline.title: Understand Ephemeral Sandbox Runs
lessonOutline.objective: Explain why ephemeral runs are separate and trace their bounded lifecycle.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner understands durable Sandbox VM commands but not ephemeral runs.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can describe Ephemeral Spec, beforeProvision hooks, Foreground Workload, afterStop hooks, cleanup, and Ephemeral Run Records.
mustCover: ADR decision: durable Sandbox Specs separate from Ephemeral Specs
`sand ephemeral init` as non-executing template generation
`sand ephemeral --from` as explicit bounded run
EphemeralRunCoordinator phases
Run record purpose
Failure/result precedence at a conceptual level
mustNotCover: Treating ephemeral as normal lifecycle command shorthand
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Order the phases of an ephemeral run and identify where to look when one phase fails.

lessonOutline.title: Debug from symptoms to tests
lessonOutline.objective: Use the test suite and repo map to diagnose behavior without guessing.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner can trace architecture but may not know where to start when something fails.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can classify a symptom, pick representative tests, inspect source, reproduce locally, and record verification evidence.
mustCover: Testing strategy table from developer guide
Symptom-to-test mapping
Targeted `swift test` vs full `make check`
Reading failures for behavior intent
Backend error translation and architecture boundary tests as guardrails
Do not weaken tests to make changes pass
mustNotCover: Blindly running every command before forming a hypothesis
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: For several failure reports, choose the first test command and first source area to inspect.

lessonOutline.title: Make a small change test-first
lessonOutline.objective: Practice a safe code change workflow using a tiny command-surface or policy example.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner can debug and navigate but has not changed behavior.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can write or update a focused test, make the smallest code change, run targeted verification, and avoid breaking boundaries.
mustCover: Start from the desired behavior and owning test file
CLICommandRouterTests for command shapes
Domain/policy tests for behavior rules
Request structs and application boundary when command shape changes
Keeping Workload Commands opaque
Running targeted tests then make check
mustNotCover: Changing docs before behavior is clear
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Draft a red-green-refactor checklist for a hypothetical tiny change, including exact files and commands.

lessonOutline.title: Know when documentation changes
lessonOutline.objective: Apply the Documentation Impact rule and refresh docs only when needed.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner can change behavior but may not understand generated docs and freshness gates.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can decide whether docs need refresh, run the correct scripts, preserve generated/managed boundaries, and avoid fake hash updates.
mustCover: Documentation Impact definition
Generated docs vs managed README sections
docs-input-manifest and generated-docs-manifest roles
scripts/generate-cli-reference.sh for CLI help changes
scripts/docs-input-hash.sh and make docs-check at a high level
Local Definition of Done from developer guide
mustNotCover: Refreshing docs for every implementation-only refactor
Docs process before learner understands behavior changes
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Classify example changes as docs-impacting or not, then choose the correct verification path.

lessonOutline.title: Integrated maintainer scenario
lessonOutline.objective: Combine user model, repo map, tracing, testing, debugging, and docs decisions on one realistic issue.
lessonOutline.enteringState: Learner has learned each skill separately.
lessonOutline.leavingState: Learner can handle a small sand issue from report to plan to verification evidence with confidence.
mustCover: Read a user symptom in product language
Reproduce or simulate the behavior path
Choose tests and source modules
Make or describe a minimal change
Run targeted and full verification
Decide Documentation Impact
Write a concise final maintainer note with evidence and limitations
mustNotCover: Revision-history or process meta-commentary in final artifacts
lessonOutline.practiceGoal: Complete a capstone triage card for a realistic sand bug or small enhancement.
